files.SKILL.md:
The first thing you do is run the tests and read the output. Not skim it — read which specific tests failed and what the assertion said. A failing test names the contract that was broken. That name is your anchor for the entire session.

When the same test failed in a previous session, the investigation is already done. Go to where the prior fix was made, read what changed, and apply the correction directly. Every exploratory step you take before looking at prior work is a step you already took before — skip it.

A bug at a boundary is not "almost right." The natural implementation is correct for all the middle cases; it fails at exactly one point. Ask: what value sits right at the limit? Reason through what the code computes there versus what it should. The error will be a single operator or a single constant, not a structural problem.

Keep your change as small as the failure demands. If a test passes, its code is not yours to touch. A patch that fixes the target failure and breaks something adjacent is worse than no patch. Look at exactly which lines the failing test exercises — change only those.

After you write the fix, confirm with the test. A run that shows the target test passing is evidence. A belief that the fix is correct is not.

When a session ends, write one sentence about the class of failure — not the details of this task, but the structural shape of what went wrong. The shape recurs. The details do not.

When asked to document a failure: name the exact failing tests, state the single line of root cause, and cite the commit where the fix landed. Anything less leaves the next person to re-discover what you already know.
